mechanism_narrative: Sustained positive growth in nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) indicates broad economic strength and rising corporate revenues, leading to upward revisions in earnings expectations and positive equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Post-2009 recovery showed strong correlation between payrolls and equity market performance.
failure_condition: The rank correlation between 3-month changes in PAYEMS and 20-day forward S&P 500 returns is not statistically significant (p > 0.05) over a 5-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp contraction in housing starts (HOUST) below a key cyclical threshold signals a severe downturn in a leading economic sector, increasing recession probability and causing negative forward returns as investors de-risk.
candidate_trigger: HOUST 3-month moving average falls below 800K units annualized.
failure_condition: When the trigger condition is met, S&P 500 60-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of instances over a full business cycle.

mechanism_narrative: When year-over-year inflation (CPIAUCSL) accelerates while the policy rate (FEDFUNDS) remains suppressed, it signals the central bank is 'behind the curve', forcing future aggressive tightening that compresses valuation multiples and generates negative returns.
candidate_trigger: YoY % change in CPIAUCSL > 3.0% while FEDFUNDS < 2.5%
failure_condition: The model's triggered signals do not have a statistically significant negative mean for 60-day forward returns (t-test p > 0.05) in out-of-sample testing.

mechanism_narrative: In a post-recession recovery, accelerating industrial production (INDPRO) indicates that supply chains are healing and corporate operating leverage is high, leading to better-than-expected earnings and positive short-term returns.
evidence_refs: fact:INDPRO growth was a strong signal during the 2010-2013 recovery phase.
failure_condition: The mechanism's predictive power (measured by rank IC) is statistically indistinguishable between post-shock recovery and late-cycle expansion regimes.

mechanism_narrative: A high unemployment rate (UNRATE) that begins to rise indicates the onset of a recessionary dynamic, leading to widespread demand destruction, corporate defaults, and sharply negative equity returns.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rises > 0.4pp from its 12-month low (Sahm Rule proxy).
failure_condition: The trigger fails to precede a NBER-defined recession in more than 2 out of 3 historical instances where it has fired.

mechanism_narrative: Strong short-term momentum in retail sales (RSAFS) reflects robust consumer spending, which directly impacts revenues for a large portion of the S&P 500 and predicts positive equity returns over a very short horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending constitutes ~70% of US GDP.
failure_condition: The 1-month change in RSAFS has a rolling 36-month correlation with 5-day forward S&P 500 returns that is consistently below 0.1.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate (UNRATE) is low and inflation (CPIAUCSL) is accelerating, it signals an overheating economy that will force the Federal Reserve to tighten policy, causing a contraction in equity multiples.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE < 5.0% and 3-month change in CPIAUCSL is positive.
evidence_refs: fact:This pattern describes the Phillips Curve trade-off which often precedes tightening cycles.
failure_condition: In periods where the trigger is active, 60-day forward returns are not negative on average with statistical significance (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: The quarter-over-quarter growth rate of real GDP (GDPC1) is the broadest measure of economic activity. A positive surprise or acceleration in this series leads to upward revisions of aggregate earnings forecasts, driving positive equity returns.
evidence_refs: fact:Equity markets are forward-looking but still react to definitive backward-looking data like GDP releases.
failure_condition: The information coefficient of quarterly GDPC1 growth surprises on subsequent 60-day S&P 500 returns is not statistically different from zero.
